refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the cycle execution capability, and (2) achieving a measured KPI (p95 ≤ CT_target). The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—what the system must do—without prescribing measurement thresholds or acceptance criteria, which belong to verification/acceptance rather than functional decomposition. The phrase 'support achieving' is softened to 'enable execution' to remain solution-neutral and problem-domain focused. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target under duty cycle and site conditions) is preserved as a verification constraint, not embedded in the FR itself.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the actual cycle time (load→travel→dump) under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Verify that the p95 percentile of observed cycle times does not exceed CT_target. This separates the functional requirement (system enables the cycle) from the acceptance criterion (measured performance meets threshold).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase to complete within the allocated time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across phases, with real-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment to maintain p95 compliance.

DP3: Design a predictive cycle-time model that accounts for site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) and pre-computes optimal phase parameters before cycle execution, with fallback to nominal timing.

DP4: Establish a closed-loop feedback control system that measures actual phase durations against budgeted targets and applies corrective actions (speed modulation, path optimization) within each phase.

DP5: Decouple cycle-time performance from external variability (site conditions, load variance) by introducing a buffering or scheduling layer that absorbs variance and guarantees deterministic phase transitions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 (integrated): Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across load, travel, and dump phases with explicit phase-duration targets. Pair this with closed-loop feedback control that monitors actual phase durations in real-time, detects deviations from budget, and applies corrective actions (e.g., speed adjustment, path re-optimization) to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability. This design separates budget planning (DP2) from execution control (DP4), enabling independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-duration targets and site-condition models: if terrain/load prediction is inaccurate, budget allocation fails. Mitigation: use conservative (pessimistic) initial budgets and adaptive re-allocation.

Feedback control loop responsiveness vs. phase duration: if corrective actions (e.g., speed change) have long latency relative to phase duration, control may be ineffective. Mitigation: ensure actuation bandwidth is ≥ 3× fastest phase duration.

Cross-phase dependencies: dump phase may be blocked if travel phase overruns; load phase may be blocked if dump phase is slow. Mitigation: design phases as independent state machines with explicit handoff protocols.
Measurement coupling: if cycle-time sensors are unreliable or have high latency, feedback control becomes unstable. Mitigation: specify sensor accuracy and latency requirements explicitly.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP4 avoids monolithic cycle design and supports independent optimization of allocation vs. control. However, success depends critically on accurate site-condition modeling and low-latency feedback. DP1 (phase decomposition) is a prerequisite; DP3 (predictive modeling) is a supporting enabler.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) are measurable or predictable with sufficient accuracy to inform initial budget allocation. RISK: If site variability is high and unpredictable, budget allocation will be conservative, reducing throughput. MITIGATION: Implement adaptive re-allocation based on first few cycles.

ASSUMPTION: Actuation system (drive motors, hydraulics, steering) can respond to corrective commands (speed, path changes) with latency ≤ 10% of phase duration. RISK: If latency is high, feedback control cannot correct deviations within a single phase. MITIGATION: Specify actuation bandwidth in DP realization; consider predictive control if latency is unavoidable.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time measurement (via sensors, GPS, or odometry) is accurate to ±5% of CT_target. RISK: If measurement noise is high, feedback control will oscillate or diverge. MITIGATION: Implement low-pass filtering and deadband logic in feedback loop.

ASSUMPTION: Load, travel, and dump phases can be executed sequentially without blocking or resource contention. RISK: If phases share actuators or hydraulic circuits, phase transitions may be delayed. MITIGATION: Validate phase independence in DP realization; design hydraulic/electrical architecture to support concurrent actuation if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined CT_target is achievable under stated duty cycle and site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic (e.g., requires speeds exceeding safety limits), no DP can satisfy FR. MITIGATION: Validate CT_target feasibility during requirements review; document assumptions about load mass, distance, and terrain.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory speed limits that make CT_target unachievable). RISK: Safety constraints may force conservative phase durations. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety team early; ensure DP respects all safety constraints.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range of site conditions (load mass, distance, terrain type) under which p95 ≤ CT_target must hold; (2) the measurement method and accuracy for cycle-time validation; (3) the priority order if CT_target conflicts with safety or operational constraints. Decompose DP2+DP4 into two separate FRs: FR2a (allocate cycle-time budget across phases) and FR2b (control phase durations in real-time to maintain budget compliance). This separation clarifies ownership and enables independent testing of budget allocation vs. feedback control. Add explicit IM layer: IM2a (cycle-time budget module, owned by planning/scheduling team) and IM2b (feedback control module, owned by vehicle control team), with a well-defined interface (phase-duration targets and actual measurements).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle-time budget allocation engine and closed-loop feedback control system that owns budget planning logic, phase-duration targets, real-time deviation detection, and corrective action orchestration across load, travel, and dump phases.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time phase duration observations, actual speeds, path metrics, and site variability signals from Vehicle to enable closed-loop feedback control and deviation detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends corrective action directives (speed adjustments, path re-optimization requests, phase-duration target updates) to Vehicle and Teleoperation to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints that bound corrective actions; sends alerts if budget allocation or feedback control decisions conflict with safety thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for mission-level optimization logic, budget planning, and closed-loop control orchestration. Budget allocation (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) are both strategic/algorithmic decisions that belong in the planning/optimization layer. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (input) and Tasking (output) cross boundary; SafetyAlert integration is localized. The separation of budget planning from execution control aligns with NGHE's role as the decision-making authority.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the execution platform, not the planning/control authority. Placing budget allocation and feedback control logic in Vehicle would scatter decision-making across the platform, create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and optimization strategy, and prevent centralized tuning of allocation and responsiveness. Corrective actions (speed, path) would be tightly coupled to vehicle internals, making strategy changes difficult. High diffusion: logic would leak into Teleoperation and NGHE Solution for coordination.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface, not an autonomous optimization engine. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are algorithmic and should not depend on operator intervention. Placing this logic in Teleoperation would create a false dependency on human presence and make autonomous operation impossible. High diffusion: logic would need to be replicated or coordinated with NGHE Solution, and Vehicle would still need independent execution capability.

Poor fit. Safety Services enforces constraints and monitors safety boundaries; it does not own mission optimization or control loops. Placing budget allocation and feedback control in Safety Services would conflate safety enforcement with performance optimization, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: NGHE Solution and Vehicle would still need independent budget/control logic, and Safety Services would become a bottleneck for every corrective action.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (budget allocation + closed-loop feedback control) is fundamentally a mission-level optimization and orchestration responsibility. NGHE Solution owns the strategic decision-making layer and is the natural authority for allocating cycle-time budgets across phases and for orchestrating corrective actions in response to real-time deviations. The separation of budget planning (DP2) from execution control (DP4) is cleanly realized within NGHE Solution as distinct algorithmic modules (budget planner and feedback controller) that share a common phase-duration target interface. Vehicle and Teleoperation are execution agents that receive directives from NGHE Solution; Safety Services is a constraint enforcer that validates NGHE decisions. This architecture preserves autonomy, enables independent tuning, and avoids scattering optimization logic across multiple modules.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if feedback control loop latency is high. Mitigation: Implement feedback control with sub-second cycle time and ensure Telemetry ingestion is non-blocking; consider edge caching of recent phase-duration observations.

Risk: Corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) may conflict with Vehicle's real-time constraints or Safety Services' boundaries. Mitigation: Define explicit action bounds in the budget allocation logic; validate all corrective actions against SafetyAlert before issuing Tasking directives.

Risk: Site variability signals may not be captured in Telemetry with sufficient fidelity to enable effective feedback control. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry schema includes environmental factors (terrain, congestion, weather) and phase-specific metrics (load mass, dump height); validate feedback control tuning against historical site data.

Risk: Separation of budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) within NGHE Solution may create coordination overhead if they operate on different timescales. Mitigation: Define clear handoff points (e.g., budget updates trigger control re-tuning) and ensure both modules share a consistent phase-duration target model.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via Telemetry ingestion of phase-duration observations. NGHE Solution's closed-loop feedback control can emit phase-duration metrics and corrective action logs that enable post-hoc verification of p95 achievement. Site variability signals (terrain, congestion, weather) must be captured in Telemetry schema to validate that control tuning is effective across the specified duty cycle.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR is allocated to a single IM boundary (NGHE Solution) with clear ownership of budget planning and feedback control orchestration. Phase-duration targets are explicit outputs of DP2 (budget allocation) and inputs to DP4 (feedback control), creating a coherent responsibility chain. Corrective actions flow through Tasking interface to Vehicle and Teleoperation; SafetyAlert provides constraint validation. No ambiguity in which module owns cycle-time optimization.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (integrated DP2 + DP4) directly addresses FR. Budget allocation mechanism distributes CT_target across load, travel, dump phases (DP2 solves the 'what budget' problem). Closed-loop feedback control monitors deviations and applies corrective actions (DP4 solves the 'how to maintain' problem under site variability). The pairing of planning and execution control is logically sound and separates concerns appropriately. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' is achievable if feedback control cycle time is sub-second and corrective action bounds are validated against SafetyAlert constraints.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Corrective action directives (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) may be rejected or delayed by Vehicle if they conflict with real-time firmware constraints or Safety Services boundaries. Mitigation: Define explicit action bounds in budget allocation logic; pre-validate all corrective actions against SafetyAlert before issuing Tasking directives. Establish latency SLA for Tasking acknowledgment.

Risk: Feedback control loop latency may exceed acceptable bounds if Telemetry ingestion, deviation detection, and Tasking issuance are not optimized. Mitigation: Implement non-blocking Telemetry ingestion with sub-second cycle time; cache recent phase-duration observations at edge to reduce round-trip latency. Monitor feedback loop latency as a KPI.

Risk: Site variability signals may not be captured with sufficient fidelity in Telemetry to enable effective feedback control tuning. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry schema to include environmental factors (terrain roughness, congestion density, weather conditions) and phase-specific metrics (load mass, dump height, path length). Validate feedback control tuning against historical site data before deployment.

Risk: Budget allocation (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) may operate on different timescales (e.g., budget updates hourly, feedback control sub-second), creating coordination overhead and potential inconsistency. Mitigation: Define explicit handoff protocol (e.g., budget updates trigger control re-tuning; feedback control respects current budget until next update). Ensure both modules share a consistent phase-duration target model with versioning.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and only appropriate home for mission-level cycle-time optimization logic. Budget allocation and feedback control are strategic/algorithmic decisions that belong in the planning/optimization layer, not in Vehicle (execution platform), Teleoperation (human interface), or Safety Services (constraint enforcer). Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (input) and Tasking (output) cross the IM boundary; SafetyAlert integration is localized and non-blocking. The separation of budget planning from execution control within NGHE Solution is cleanly realized as distinct algorithmic modules sharing a common phase-duration target interface. Vehicle and Teleoperation are execution agents; Safety Services is a constraint validator. This architecture preserves autonomy, enables independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness, and avoids scattering optimization logic across multiple modules.

recommended_revisions: Extend Telemetry interface specification to explicitly include site variability signals (terrain type/roughness, congestion density, weather conditions, load mass, dump height, path length) required for effective feedback control tuning. Validate that Vehicle telemetry schema captures these factors with sufficient fidelity.

Define explicit action bounds and validation protocol for corrective actions (speed adjustment range, path re-optimization constraints) in budget allocation logic. Establish pre-validation against SafetyAlert before Tasking issuance to prevent conflicts and reduce rejection rate.

Specify feedback control loop latency SLA (target: sub-second cycle time) and implement non-blocking Telemetry ingestion with edge caching of recent phase-duration observations to minimize round-trip latency. Monitor feedback loop latency as a KPI during integration testing.

Define handoff protocol between budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) modules to handle different timescales (e.g., budget updates trigger control re-tuning; feedback control respects current budget until next update). Ensure both modules share a versioned phase-duration target model.

Establish acceptance test plan that validates p95 ≤ CT_target across the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Include test scenarios covering site variability (terrain, congestion, weather) and corrective action effectiveness (speed adjustment, path re-optimization). Validate feedback control tuning against historical site data before production deployment.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence and excellent containment (0.92 score). The FR is well-scoped, the DP logically addresses it, and the IM boundary is appropriate and minimal. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that several critical sub-problems remain unresolved at the current level: (1) Telemetry schema completeness for site variability capture, (2) corrective action bounds validation and SafetyAlert pre-validation protocol, (3) feedback control loop latency SLA and edge caching architecture, (4) handoff protocol between budget planning and feedback control operating at different timescales, and (5) acceptance test plan covering duty cycle and site variability. These are not defects in the current FR-DP-IM mapping but rather dependencies that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure implementation feasibility and risk mitigation. Proceeding to the next level will expose the architectural and algorithmic details needed to validate the diffusion risks and confirm that the DP can be realized within safety and performance constraints.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Capture and ingest site variability signals (terrain type/roughness, congestion density, weather conditions, load mass, dump height, path length) from Vehicle Telemetry with sufficient fidelity to enable feedback control tuning.
FR2.2: Validate corrective action directives (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) against SafetyAlert constraints and action bounds before issuing Tasking commands to Vehicle.

FR2.3: Execute feedback control loop with sub-second cycle time, including non-blocking Telemetry ingestion, deviation detection, and Tasking issuance, with edge caching of recent phase-duration observations to minimize latency.
FR2.4: Coordinate budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) modules operating at different timescales via explicit handoff protocol and shared versioned phase-duration target model.

FR2.5: Verify that p95 cycle-time ≤ CT_target is achieved across the agreed duty cycle and site conditions through acceptance testing that covers terrain, congestion, weather variability and validates corrective action effectiveness.

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR, DP, and IM are sound. Decomposition to sub-FRs will address the open risks and recommended revisions.

assumptions: Vehicle Telemetry interface can be extended to capture site variability signals (terrain, congestion, weather, load, dump height, path length) with sub-second update frequency.
SafetyAlert constraint validation can be performed synchronously within feedback control loop without exceeding sub-second latency budget.
Corrective action bounds (speed adjustment range, path re-optimization constraints) can be pre-computed and validated offline before deployment.
Budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) can operate independently with explicit handoff protocol and shared phase-duration target model without creating race conditions or inconsistency.
Feedback control loop latency (Telemetry ingestion + deviation detection + Tasking issuance) can be reduced to sub-second via edge caching and non-blocking I/O.
Historical site data is available to validate feedback control tuning before production deployment.
open_risks: Telemetry schema may not capture site variability with sufficient fidelity; feedback control tuning may be ineffective if environmental factors are missing or noisy.

Corrective action directives may be rejected or delayed by Vehicle firmware if they conflict with real-time constraints or Safety Services boundaries; rejection rate and latency impact on p95 achievement unknown.
Feedback control loop latency may exceed sub-second target if Telemetry ingestion, deviation detection, or Tasking issuance are not optimized; round-trip latency SLA not yet established.

Budget planning and feedback control operating at different timescales (e.g., hourly budget updates vs. sub-second feedback control) may create coordination overhead and inconsistency; handoff protocol not yet defined.
Acceptance test plan does not yet cover all duty cycle and site variability scenarios; p95 achievement may not be validated across full operational envelope before production deployment.

Safety criticality is high; any failure in corrective action validation or feedback control loop could impact vehicle safety. Integration with SafetyAlert and Vehicle firmware must be validated thoroughly.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification with site variability signals (terrain type/roughness, congestion density, weather conditions, load mass, dump height, path length) and validation that Vehicle can emit these metrics with required fidelity and frequency.
Corrective action bounds specification (speed adjustment range, path re-optimization constraints) and pre-validation protocol against SafetyAlert constraints.
Feedback control loop latency measurement and SLA (target: sub-second cycle time); edge caching architecture and non-blocking I/O implementation plan.
Handoff protocol specification between budget planning and feedback control modules; versioned phase-duration target model and synchronization mechanism.

Acceptance test plan covering duty cycle scenarios (load, travel, dump phases), site variability (terrain, congestion, weather), and corrective action effectiveness (speed adjustment, path re-optimization); historical site data for tuning validation.
Integration test results validating feedback control loop latency, corrective action acceptance rate, and p95 cycle-time achievement under agreed duty cycle and site conditions.
Safety analysis confirming that corrective action validation and feedback control loop failures do not violate safety constraints; SafetyAlert integration validation.
